title: Enterospermum madagascariense
taxonomy: Enterospermum madagascariense is classified as a genus of extinct plants in the family Zamiaceae, a group of seed plants that are commonly known as cycads. These plants are part of the ancient lineage that is closely related to the modern cycads. They belong to the division Pinophyta, which includes conifers and other seed-producing plants. As members of the class Cycadopsida, they are distinct from other seed plants due to their unique characteristics. Their classification also places them within the phylum Spermatophyta, which comprises all seed-producing plants.

intro: Enterospermum madagascariense, also known as Madagascar Periwinkle, is a plant species native to the island of Madagascar, belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections, due to its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antimalarial properties. The bioactive constituents of Enterospermum madagascariense include vinblastine and vincristine, alkaloids with cytotoxic and antitumor activities, as well as other compounds like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Herbal preparations made from Enterospermum madagascariense include teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are often used to treat fever, skin conditions, and respiratory issues, although its use should be done under medical supervision due to potential toxicity.
